species_page.contribute_title: Help keep this species up to date
species_page.contribute_text: Found new data or photos? Send a suggestion for curator review.

NE.name: Not Evaluated
NE.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means the species has not yet been assessed under the criteria.

DD.name: Data Deficient
DD.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means there is not enough information to assess the species’ risk of extinction.

LC.name: Least Concern
LC.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means the species has been evaluated and is not currently considered at significant risk of extinction.

NT.name: Near Threatened
NT.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means the species is not yet threatened, but is close to qualifying as threatened.

VU.name: Vulnerable
VU.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means the species faces a high risk of extinction in the wild.

EN.name: Endangered
EN.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means the species faces a very high risk of extinction in the wild.

CR.name: Critically Endangered
CR.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means the species faces an extremely high risk of extinction in the wild.

EW.name: Extinct in the Wild
EW.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means the species survives only in captivity, cultivation, or outside its natural historical range.

EX.name: Extinct
EX.description: In the IUCN Red List, this means there is no reasonable doubt that the last individual has died.

about_page.title: About
about_page.paragraph1: The LUMM (Luminescent Mushroom Database) is a scientific repository dedicated to collecting, organizing, and sharing information about bioluminescent fungi—organisms capable of producing natural light through biochemical reactions.
about_page.paragraph2: All known bioluminescent fungi are Basidiomycetes belonging to the Agaricales order. They emit green light with wavelengths between 520–530 nm, often following a circadian rhythm that allows them to glow continuously throughout the day and night.
about_page.paragraph3: Over the last 15 years, the number of known bioluminescent fungal species has more than doubled, increasing from 64 to 132 species. These species are grouped into five main evolutionary lineages within the Agaricales:
about_page.lineages_list:
- Omphalotaceae (18 species)
- Physalacriaceae (14 species)
- Mycenaceae (96 species)
- Linhagem Lucentipes (3 species)
- Cyphellopsidaceae (1 species)
about_page.paragraph4: Bioluminescent fungi are distributed worldwide, with the highest diversity found in subtropical forests rich in plant life, particularly in regions such as Japan, South America, North America, and Southeast Asia.
about_page.paragraph5: The LUMM database integrates data on taxonomy, morphology, ecology, geographic distribution, and genomic and transcriptomic information from sequenced species. This platform supports researchers studying fungal evolution, ecology, and synthetic biology, providing a centralized source for comparative and phylogenetic analyses.
about_page.paragraph6: Recent studies, such as those by Kotlobay et al. (2023), have uncovered the biochemical and genetic basis of fungal bioluminescence. The light emission results from a Caffeic Acid Cycle (CAC), where the metabolite 3-hydroxyhispidin acts as the fungal luciferin. The process involves four main enzymes, including luciferase (luz), encoded by a conserved gene cluster found in all bioluminescent fungi.
about_page.paragraph7: This cluster likely evolved once early in the history of the Agaricales, with later gene duplication events generating new functional variants. The luciferase gene itself represents a novel protein family, with no known homologs in other organisms.
about_page.paragraph8: By compiling ecological, genetic, and biochemical data, LUMM serves as a valuable tool for understanding how and why certain mushrooms glow in the dark—and how this remarkable trait evolved in nature.
about_page.reference1: Perry, B. A., Desjardin, D. E., & Stevani, C. V. (2024). Diversity, Distribution, and Evolution of Bioluminescent Fungi. Journal of Fungi, 11(1), 19.
about_page.reference2: Kotlobay, A. A., Sarkisyan, K. S., Mokrushina, Y. A., Marcet-Houben, M., Serebrovskaya, E. O., Markina, N. M., ... & Yampolsky, I. V. (2018). Genetically encodable bioluminescent system from fungi. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 115(50), 12728-12732.

glossary_page.title: Glossary
glossary_page.paragraph1: This glossary provides definitions for terms that are useful in understanding zebrafish development, anatomy, genetics and bioinformatics. Terms are defined in a general sense as they apply to eukaryotic genetics, particularly zebrafish genetics; some terms are also defined as they are specifically used in ZFIN (e.g., gene). This list will grow as time goes on. If there is a term you think should be included in this list, please contact ZFIN User Support.
glossary_page.paragraph2: The terms are alphabetically ordered across all categories. Categories: [Morphology] based on Vellinga (Flora Agaricina Neerlandica, Cap. 2 and 8); [Ecology] based on Põlme et al. 2021 (FungalTraits); [Bioluminescence] based on Kaskova et al. 2017, Purtov et al. 2015 and subsequent literature; [Collection] based on Lodge et al. 2004 and IAPT nomenclature standards.
glossary_page.paragraph3: Click on a letter below to navigate through the taxonomy and morphology terms in the LUMM Glossary.
glossary_page.3-Hydroxyhispidin_description: The fungal luciferin; the light-emitting substrate oxidised by luciferase. A hydroxylated derivative of hispidin, produced from hispidin by hispidin-3-hydroxylase (H3H). First identified by Purtov et al. (2015). λmax of emission ~520 nm (green).
glossary_page.Abrupt_papilla_description: A small, nipple-like protuberance on the pileus with an abrupt, not gradual, base. Distinct from acute papilla, which tapers to a point. (Vellinga, Fig. 29.30)
glossary_page.Acuminate_description: (Of cystidia) tapering from inwardly curved sides to a point.(Vellinga, Fig. 34.39)
glossary_page.Acute_papilla_description: A sharp-pointed papilla on the pileus apex.
glossary_page.Adnate_description: (Of lamellae) broadly attached to the stipe horizontally, with the full depth of the gill making contact. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.21)
glossary_page.Adnexed_description: (Of lamellae) rounded toward the stipe with only the upper part making contact. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.19)
glossary_page.Allantoid_description: (Of spores) sausage-shaped; elongated and slightly curved. (Vellinga, Fig. 33.21)
glossary_page.Amyloid_description: (Of spore wall, hyphal walls) staining greyish to blue-black in Melzer's reagent. Indicates presence of starch-like polysaccharides.
glossary_page.Anastomosing_description: (Of lamellae) provided with irregular transverse connections between gill faces. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.4)
glossary_page.Angiocarpy_description: A type of basidiocarp development in which the hymenium is enclosed in a cavity at some stages; includes primary and secondary angiocarpy.
glossary_page.Annulus_description: Ring-like remnant of the partial veil on the stipe, formed after veil breakage. See types: simple, double, ascending, pendant, cog-wheel, peronate.
glossary_page.Aquatic_habitat_description: In FungalTraits: a categorical field indicating whether a fungal taxon inhabits aquatic environments. States: non-aquatic, freshwater, marine, partly aquatic, brackish.
glossary_page.Arbuscular_mycorrhizal_AM_description: A mutualistic symbiosis between plant roots and Glomeromycota fungi, characterised by intracellular arbuscular structures and non-septate hyphae. Not known in any bioluminescent fungal lineage.
glossary_page.Arcuate_description: (Of lamellae) with a concave lamella edge. (Vellinga, Figs. 30.15, 30.16)
glossary_page.Areolate_rimose_description: (Of pileus surface) marked with numerous superficial clefts or cracks forming angular patches.
glossary_page.Ascending_description: (Of hyphae) curving upward. Also a type of annulus curving upward from attachment point.
glossary_page.Basidiocarp_description: The fruiting body of a basidiomycete fungus that produces spores on basidia. Synonymous with basidiome.
glossary_page.Basidiole_description: An immature basidium before sterigmata development.
glossary_page.Basidiome_age_class_description: Classification of a fruiting body by developmental stage: young (unexpanded, veil intact), mature (pileus expanded, spores produced), or old/senescent (pileus degrading, spore production declining). Record age class at collection for all measurements.
glossary_page.Binding_hyphae_description: Branching, rarely septate, thick-walled, narrow hyphae binding other hyphal elements together in dimitic or trimitic tissue systems.
glossary_page.Bioluminescence_term_description: The emission of light by a living organism as a result of a chemical reaction, without external light input. In fungi: enzymatic oxidation of 3-hydroxyhispidin by luciferase. Distinct from fluorescence (re-emission of absorbed light) and phosphorescence.
glossary_page.Biotroph_description: An organism that obtains nutrients from living host tissue without killing it. Includes mutualists (e.g. mycorrhizal fungi) and obligate parasites.
glossary_page.Brown_rot_description: Wood decay type in which cellulose and hemicellulose are degraded while lignin remains largely intact, leaving a brown, cubically cracked residue. No bioluminescent species are known to cause brown rot.
glossary_page.Bulbous_description: (Of stipe base) enlarged abruptly or gradually. See marginately bulbous, abruptly bulbous. (Vellinga, Fig. 31.17)
glossary_page.Caffeic_acid_description: The starting compound of the fungal bioluminescence recycling pathway. Converted to hispidin by HispS (using malonyl-CoA). Regenerated from caffeylpyruvate (oxyluciferin) by caffeylpyruvate hydrolase (CPH), enabling cyclic luciferin recycling.
glossary_page.Caffeylpyruvate_description: The fungal oxyluciferin; the product of luciferase-catalysed oxidation of 3-hydroxyhispidin. Hydrolysed by caffeylpyruvate hydrolase (CPH) to caffeic acid + pyruvate, completing the recycling loop.
glossary_page.Caffeylpyruvate_hydrolase_CPH_descriptio: The fourth enzyme of the fungal bioluminescence pathway. Hydrolyses caffeylpyruvate (oxyluciferin) back to caffeic acid and pyruvate, enabling recycling of the luciferin precursor. Gene: cph.
glossary_page.Capitate_description: (Of cystidia) having a distinct and abrupt knob at the apex. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.44)
glossary_page.Carbonicolous_description: growing on burned ground or charcoal
glossary_page.Caulocystidia_description: Cystidium situated on the surface of the stipe. Morphology often matches or contrasts with lamellar cystidia.
glossary_page.Cheilocystidia_description: Cystidium situated on the edge of lamella or tube. Key identification feature in Mycena, Marasmius, Galerina, and many other genera.
glossary_page.Chrysocystidia_description: Cystidium with yellow amorphous body or bodies in contents, visible after treatment with NH₄OH or KOH. Found in Hypholoma, Stropharia, some Pholiota.
glossary_page.Circadian_rhythm_bioluminescence_descrip: A 24-hour cycle of bioluminescence intensity in some fungi (e.g. Neonothopanus gardneri), where luminescence is brighter at night and dimmer during the day, regulated by temperature oscillation rather than a dedicated circadian clock (Oliveira et al. 2015).
glossary_page.Clamp_connection_description: A bypass connection at a septum in dikaryotic hyphae ensuring proper nuclear distribution during cell division. Presence/absence is taxonomically significant.
glossary_page.Clavate_description: Club-shaped; wider toward the apex. (Of stipe: Vellinga, Fig. 31.8; of cystidia: Fig. 34.13)
glossary_page.Clitocyboid_description: (Of habit) characterized by plano-convex to deeply infundibuliform pileus; lamellae decurrent to broadly adnate; stipe continuous with pileus context.
glossary_page.Collarium_description: A tube around, but free from, the apex of the stipe to which lamellae are attached. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.5)
glossary_page.Collection_event_description: A single collecting episode at a defined location and date, yielding one or more specimen records. Multiple basidiomes collected from the same substrate in the same visit may constitute one collection event with multiple vouchers.
glossary_page.Conical_description: (Of pileus) cone-shaped. Includes broadly conical, narrowly conical, truncately broadly conical. (Vellinga, Fig. 29.14)
glossary_page.Connective_hyphae_description: Usually narrow, undifferentiated hyphae connecting all elements of a basidiocarp context.
glossary_page.Constriction_description: Transverse narrowing in the middle of spores or cystidia. See median constriction.
glossary_page.Context_description: The internal flesh of the pileus and stipe, excluding the hymenophore tissue. Colour, consistency, and reactions (especially to cut surfaces) are diagnostic.
glossary_page.Corticolous_description: growing on the bark of living trees or shrubs
glossary_page.Cortina_description: A web-like partial veil of fine fibrils connecting pileus margin to stipe; characteristic of Cortinarius. Cobwebby appearance.
glossary_page.Crenate_description: (Of margin or lamella edge) with rounded teeth. (Vellinga, Fig. 29.45)
glossary_page.Cutis_description: A pileipellis consisting of repent (horizontal), non-gelatinising hyphae. The most common pileipellis type. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.1A)
glossary_page.Cystidium_pl_cystidia_description: Sterile, differentiated terminal element in the hymenium or on the surfaces of the basidiocarp. Types distinguished by location (cheilocystidia, pleurocystidia, etc.) and shape.
glossary_page.Decay_substrate_description: In FungalTraits: the substrate in or on which a saprotrophic fungus decomposes organic matter. States include dead wood, leaf litter, soil, dung, herbaceous material, and others.
glossary_page.Decurrent_description: (Of lamellae) descending down the stipe. Angle of lamella–stipe junction > 40°. (Vellinga, Figs. 30.25, 30.28)
glossary_page.Deliquescent_description: (Of lamellae or basidiocarp) becoming liquid as it matures. Characteristic of Coprinus and related genera (Coprinellus, Coprinopsis).
glossary_page.Dextrinoid_description: (Of spore wall, hyphal walls) staining red to reddish-brown in Melzer's reagent.
glossary_page.Dimitic_description: (Of tissue) consisting of generative hyphae and either skeletal or binding hyphae. More rigid than monomitic tissue.
glossary_page.Divergent_description: (Of hymenophoral trama) having hyphae turning outward from a median line. Two types: Hygrophorus-type and Amanita-type. (Vellinga, Fig. 35.5, 35.6)
glossary_page.Eccentric_description: (Of stipe) not attached to the centre of the pileus. (Vellinga, Fig. 31.2)
glossary_page.Ectomycorrhiza_exploration_type_descript: Classification of EcM fungi by the morphology of their extraradical mycelium and rhizomorphs, reflecting nutrient foraging strategy (Tedersoo & Smith 2013). Types: contact, short-distance delicate, short-distance coarse, medium-distance smooth/fringe/mat, long-distance.
glossary_page.Ectomycorrhizal_EcM_description: A mutualistic symbiosis between plant roots and fungi in which the fungal mantle covers the root tip and a Hartig net penetrates between epidermal cells without entering them. Some bioluminescent Armillaria and Omphalotus species occur near but are not themselves EcM — they are saprotrophic or parasitic.
glossary_page.Emarginate_description: (Of lamellae) notched near the stipe. Synonymous with sinuate. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.23)
glossary_page.En_brosse_description: (Of cystidia) with excrescences, diverticulate; brush-like. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.45)
glossary_page.Endophyte_description: A fungus that lives within plant tissue (asymptomatic, commensal, or weakly mutualistic). Classified into four classes by Rodriguez et al. (2009).
glossary_page.Endosporium_description: The electron-transparent inner layer of the basidiospore wall, inside the episporium, absent in many white-spored taxa.
glossary_page.Entire_description: (Of lamella edge or pileus margin) straight, smooth, and glabrous. (Vellinga, Figs. 30.30, 30.38)
glossary_page.Episporium_description: The electron-opaque fundamental layer of the basidiospore wall; the innermost layer of the spore wall when endosporium is absent.
glossary_page.Epithelioid_hymeniderm_description: A hymeniderm made up of elements with Q = 1.0–1.15; nearly isodiametric. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.3C)
glossary_page.Epithelium_description: A pileipellis made up of globose to broadly ellipsoid elements in more than one layer. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.4A, 4B)
glossary_page.Euhymeniderm_description: A hymeniderm made up of elements with Q = 1.15–6. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.8)
glossary_page.Exsiccata_description: A properly dried and labelled herbarium specimen prepared from a fresh collection. The physical voucher that makes a record verifiable.
glossary_page.Fasciculate_description: (Of basidiocarps) growing in a bundle or tight cluster, bases often fused.
glossary_page.Filiform_description: (Of cystidia) very long and narrowly cylindrical.
glossary_page.Fimbriate_description: (Of lamella edge) with regular hair-like projections. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.39)
glossary_page.Fistulose_description: (Of stipe) hollow with a very narrow or thread-like central cavity. (Vellinga, Fig. 31.11)
glossary_page.Floccose_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) covered with tufts of soft hairs; cottony patches.
glossary_page.Foliar_endophyte_description: Endophyte inhabiting above-ground plant tissue (leaves, stems). One of the most species-rich fungal guilds.
glossary_page.Free_description: (Of lamellae) not attached to stipe. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.18)
glossary_page.Fusiform_description: Spindle-shaped, tapering at both ends. (Of spores: Q = 2.0–4.0; of cystidia: Q = 2.0–4.0.) (Vellinga, Figs. 33.13, 34.20)
glossary_page.Generative_hyphae_description: The basic type of septate, thin- to thick-walled branching hyphae, present in all basidiocarps, from which all differentiated hyphae arise.
glossary_page.Glabrous_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) bald; without hairs, scales, or ornamentation.
glossary_page.Granulose_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) covered with (or composed of) minute grains.
glossary_page.Gregarious_description: (Of basidiocarps) growing in a group, several individuals in proximity but not fused at base.
glossary_page.Guild_description: A group of species that exploit the same class of environmental resources in a similar way, regardless of taxonomic relationship. In mycology: saprotrophs, mycorrhizal fungi, pathogens, etc.
glossary_page.Gymnocarpy_description: A type of development of the basidiocarp in which the hymenium is exposed from initiation until maturity.
glossary_page.Hemibiotroph_description: An organism that initially grows biotrophically (in living tissue) and later switches to necrotrophy (killing host cells). Common strategy in plant pathogens.
glossary_page.Hemispherical_description: (Of pileus) with shape of a half sphere. (Vellinga, Fig. 29.10)
glossary_page.Herbarium_voucher_description: A dried specimen deposited in a recognised herbarium collection, assigned a unique accession number. Vouchers allow independent re-examination of morphological claims.
glossary_page.Heteromerous_description: (Of tissue) consisting of hyphae and 'nests' of spherocysts. Present in Russula and Lactarius.
glossary_page.Hilar_appendage_description: Short process at basal end of spore, by which it was attached to sterigma. Shape varies: simple, nodulose type, open pore type.
glossary_page.Hispid description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) covered with long or short, erect, stiff hairs or bristles.
glossary_page.Hispidin_description: The precursor of the fungal luciferin. A polyketide (styrylpyrone) biosynthesised from caffeic acid by hispidin synthase (HispS). Also found as an antioxidant in non-bioluminescent fungi. Hydroxylated by H3H to form 3-hydroxyhispidin (luciferin).
glossary_page.Hispidin_synthase_HispS_description: The first enzyme of the fungal bioluminescence pathway. A type I polyketide synthase (~5.1 kb gene) that condenses caffeic acid with two malonyl-CoA units to produce hispidin. Requires post-translational phosphopantetheinylation by NpgA for activity. Gene: hisps.
glossary_page.Hispidin_3_hydroxylase_H3H_description: The second enzyme of the fungal bioluminescence pathway. An NAD(P)H-dependent oxidase that converts hispidin to 3-hydroxyhispidin (fungal luciferin). Requires molecular oxygen. Gene: h3h or nnH3H.
glossary_page.Holotype_description: The single specimen designated (or indicated) by the author of a species as the nomenclatural type at the time of original publication. Only one holotype exists per accepted name.
glossary_page.Hymeniderm_description: A derm made up of non-septate elements originating at the same level. See also epithelioid hymeniderm and euhymeniderm.
glossary_page.Hymenium_description: The spore-bearing layer of the basidiocarp, comprising basidia, basidioles, and often cystidia.
glossary_page.Hymenophoral_trama_description: The tissue of the hymenophore between the two hymenial layers. Type (regular, irregular, divergent) is taxonomically important. (Vellinga, Fig. 35)
glossary_page.Irregular_epithelium_description: An epithelium made up of irregularly disposed elements. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.4B)
glossary_page.Isotype_description: A duplicate specimen of the holotype, collected simultaneously and from the same individual or population.
glossary_page.Ixocutis_description: A cutis made up of gelatinising hyphae; the matrix becomes gelatinous. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.1B)
glossary_page.Lacrymoid_description: (Of spores) with confluent hilar appendage; tear-shaped. (Vellinga, Figs. 33.16, 33.17)
glossary_page.Lageniform_description: (Of cystidia) flask-shaped; characterised by a neck narrower than half the width of the cell body. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.25)
glossary_page.Lecythiform_description: (Of cystidia) lageniform and abruptly capitate. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.33)
glossary_page.Litter_saprotroph_description: A saprotrophic fungus that decomposes dead plant material in the surface litter layer, primarily cellulose and hemicellulose. Many bioluminescent Mycena and Panellus are litter saprotrophs.
glossary_page.Luciferase_fungal_Luz_description: The enzyme that catalyses light emission in bioluminescent fungi. Oxidises 3-hydroxyhispidin with molecular oxygen, producing an α-pyrone endoperoxide intermediate that decomposes to caffeylpyruvate (oxyluciferin) and a photon (~520 nm). An insoluble enzyme requiring no cofactors beyond O₂. Gene: luz or nnLuz.
glossary_page.Luciferin_description: The substrate of a luciferase reaction that is oxidised to produce light. In fungi: 3-hydroxyhispidin. Distinct from luciferins of other bioluminescent systems (coelenterazine in marine organisms; D-luciferin in fireflies).
glossary_page.Luminescence_description: Emission of light without significant heat, by any mechanism. Bioluminescence is the biological form; chemiluminescence is the purely chemical form. Fungal bioluminescence is a chemiluminescent reaction catalysed by a biological enzyme.
glossary_page.Marginately_bulbous_description: (Of stipe base) provided with a bulb with a raised border. (Vellinga, Fig. 31.19)
glossary_page.Medallion_clamp_description: A clamp connection with an opening between the clamp connection itself and the elements connected by it.
glossary_page.Median_constriction_description: Transverse contraction in the middle of spores or cystidia. (Vellinga, Figs. 33.24, 33.25, 34.30)
glossary_page.Moniliform_description: (Of cystidia) cylindrical but contracted at regular intervals, like a string of beads. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.35)
glossary_page.Monomitic_description: (Of tissue) built up of one type of hyphae (generative hyphae only). The simplest and most common tissue system in Agaricales.
glossary_page.Mucronate_description: (Of cystidia) with small abrupt, acute, or blunt protuberance at apex. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.37)
glossary_page.Mycenoid_description: (Of habit) characterised by pileus conical to paraboloid; lamellae free to adnate; stipe usually long and slender; context usually brittle.
glossary_page.Mycoparasite_description: A fungus parasitic on other fungi. Some species in FungalTraits primary lifestyle.
glossary_page.Narrowly_adnate_description: (Of lamellae) with only the very top part of the gill depth attaching to the stipe. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.20)
glossary_page.Necrotroph_description: An organism that kills host cells and feeds on the dead tissue. Contrasts with biotroph and distinguishes from hemibiotroph.
glossary_page.NpgA_phosphopantetheinyl_transferase_des: A post-translational modifier required for HispS (hispidin synthase) activity. Attaches a phosphopantetheine group to HispS, activating it as an acyl carrier protein. Gene loss or truncation correlates with loss of bioluminescence in some Armillaria lineages.
glossary_page.Obovoid_description: (Of spores/cystidia) reversely ovoid, broadest and widest part uppermost. (Vellinga, Fig. 33.9)
glossary_page.Omphalinoid_description: (Of habit) characterised by plano-convex to deeply infundibuliform pileus; lamellae decurrent; context of pileus continuous with stipe.
glossary_page.Ovoid_description: Egg-shaped; broadest below the middle. (Vellinga, Fig. 33.8)
glossary_page.Oxyluciferin_fungal_description: The product of luciferin oxidation by luciferase; in fungi this is caffeylpyruvate. Released in an electronically excited state, it emits a photon (~520 nm) upon returning to the ground state. Recycled by CPH.
glossary_page.Papilla_description: Small nipple-like protuberance on pileus or spore apex.
glossary_page.Paraboloid_description: (Of pileus) with the curve of a parabola; narrower than hemispherical but broader than conical. (Vellinga, Fig. 29.11)
glossary_page.Paratype_description: A specimen cited in the original publication other than the holotype or isotype, used to supplement the type description.
glossary_page.Peak_emission_wavelength_max_description: The wavelength at which a bioluminescent emission spectrum shows maximum intensity. For the fungal hispidin pathway: ~520 nm (green light). Determined by spectrophotometry on fresh tissue or purified luciferin-luciferase system.
glossary_page.Pileipellis_description: Cortical layer(s) of the pileus. Structure type (cutis, trichoderm, epithelium, hymeniderm, etc.) is a primary identification character. (Vellinga, Fig. 36)
glossary_page.Pileus_description: The cap of an agaric; the expanded, spore-bearing portion of the basidiocarp. Supports the hymenophore on its undersurface.
glossary_page.Pleurocystidia_description: Cystidium situated on the sides (face) of lamella or tube. Absent in many genera but diagnostically important when present.
glossary_page.Pleurotoid_description: (Of habit) characterised by stipe absent or lateral; pileus attached laterally to substrate. Includes Pleurotus, Hohenbuehelia, Crepidotus.
glossary_page.Pluteoid_description: (Of habit) characterised by lamellae free; context of pileus discontinuous with context of stipe; stipe usually longer than pileus diameter.
glossary_page.Primary_lifestyle_description: In FungalTraits: the most commonly occurring lifestyle of a fungal genus, represented by 30 character states. Used as the primary trophic classification.
glossary_page.Protologue_description: The entire material published with a new name at the time of its original publication, including the description, diagnosis, and any illustrations. The protologue defines the circumscription of the taxon.
glossary_page.Pruinose_description: (Of pileus, lamella, and stipe surfaces) covered with a (often white or whitish) powdery bloom.
glossary_page.Pseudorrhiza_description: A root-like extension of the stipe going deep into the substrate. (Vellinga, Fig. 31.15)
glossary_page.Pubescent_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) = downy; covered with short, soft, fine hairs.
glossary_page.Pulverulent_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) covered with powder.
glossary_page.Q_ratio_description: The quotient of spore length (L) divided by width (W). A dimensionless index of spore elongation. Q < 1.15 = globose; 1.15–1.3 = broadly ellipsoid; 1.3–1.6 = ellipsoid; 1.6–2.0 = oblong; > 2.0 = elongated (cylindrical, fusiform, etc.).
glossary_page.Quantum_yield_description: The ratio of photons emitted to luciferin molecules oxidised. In fungal bioluminescence: relatively low quantum yield compared to other bioluminescent systems, which is one reason luminescence is most visible in full darkness.
glossary_page.Reactive_oxygen_species_ROS_description: Chemically reactive molecules containing oxygen (O₂⁻, H₂O₂, OH•). Evidence suggests that environmental ROS may regulate bioluminescence intensity in some fungal species, acting as upstream signals affecting gene expression of the luz cluster.
glossary_page.Regular_epithelium_description: An epithelium made up of elements in erect rows. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.4A)
glossary_page.Repent_description: (Of hyphae) creeping, not ascending. The defining feature of a cutis-type pileipellis.
glossary_page.Rhizomorph_description: A visible root-like mycelial strand; macroscopic aggregation of hyphae oriented along a growth axis. Prominent and ecologically important in Armillaria and other rhizomorph-producing genera. (Vellinga, Fig. 31.14)
glossary_page.Rimose_description: (Of pileus surface) marked with numerous superficial radial cracks. See also areolate-rimose.
glossary_page.Ripicolous_description: associated with riverbanks or very moist habitats
glossary_page.Rostrate_description: (Of cystidia) provided with a beak-like extension at apex. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.38)
glossary_page.Saprotroph_description: An organism that obtains nutrients from dead organic matter. The dominant trophic mode in Agaricales. Subtypes in FungalTraits: wood saprotroph, litter saprotroph, soil saprotroph, dung saprotroph, nectar/sap saprotroph, pollen saprotroph, unspecified saprotroph.
glossary_page.Sarcodimitic_description: （Of tissue) consisting of generative hyphae and chains of very long, thin- to thick-walled elements ('sarcoskeletals').
glossary_page.Seceding_description: (Of lamellae) at first attached to stipe, later separating. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.22)
glossary_page.Secondary_lifestyle_description: In FungalTraits: one or more additional lifestyles of a genus, relevant when the genus exhibits significant lifestyle variation.
glossary_page.Segmentiform_description: (Of lamellae) with straight lamella edge and convex upper side. (Vellinga, Figs. 30.7, 30.8, 30.9)
glossary_page.Septate_description: (Of hyphae) with cross-walls (septa). All generative hyphae are septate.
glossary_page.Sericeous_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) silky; covered with fine, straight, adpressed, glossy hairs or fibrils.
glossary_page.Serrate_description: (Of lamella edge) toothed like a saw. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.35)
glossary_page.Sessile_description: (Of basidiocarps or cystidia) without a stalk. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.47)
glossary_page.Sinuate_description: (Of lamellae) having a concave indentation near the stipe. Synonymous with emarginate. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.24)
glossary_page.Skeletal_hyphae_description: Aseptate, thick-walled, straight or slightly flexuous hyphae providing structural support. Component of dimitic and trimitic systems.
glossary_page.Solitary_description: (Of basidiocarps) single, with one individual at a time.
glossary_page.Species_Hypothesis_SH_description: In UNITE: a species-level cluster of ITS sequences grouped at 1% dissimilarity threshold, assigned a permanent digital object identifier (DOI). The operational unit for molecular fungal diversity comparisons. Format: SH1234567.08FU.
glossary_page.Spectrophotometry_bioluminescence_descri: Measurement of the wavelength and intensity of light emitted by bioluminescent tissue using a spectrophotometer or spectrometer. Used to determine λmax and compare emission profiles between species or tissues.
glossary_page.Squamulose_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) covered with minute scales. Distinguished from squamose (larger scales).
glossary_page.Squarrose_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) covered with projecting coarse scales, often recurved.
glossary_page.Stipe_description: The stem or stalk of an agaric basidiocarp; the supporting structure bearing the pileus.
glossary_page.Striate_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) marked with regular lines. On pileus, often caused by visibility of underlying gill edges through the cap flesh.
glossary_page.Strigose_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) covered with long, coarse or thick, rather stiff hairs.
glossary_page.Stuffed_description: (Of stipe) having a central part filled with tissue different from the outer cortex. (Vellinga, Fig. 31.10)
glossary_page.Suprahilar_depression_description: (Of spores) sinking just above the hilar appendage. (Vellinga, Fig. 32.4B)
glossary_page.Suprahilar_plage_description: (Of spores) rounded, smooth area just above the hilar appendage. (Vellinga, Fig. 32.5D)
glossary_page.Symbiotroph_description: An organism that obtains nutrients through a symbiotic relationship with a living host, without necessarily being mutualistic (e.g. parasitic and mycorrhizal interactions are both symbiotrophy).
glossary_page.Syntype_description: Any specimen cited in the original description when no holotype was designated. Syntypes have equal nomenclatural standing; one is typically later designated as the lectotype.
glossary_page.Tibiiform_description: (Of cystidia) lageniform with long neck and capitate apex. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.34)
glossary_page.Tomentose_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) densely covered with matted (more or less adpressed) soft hairs.
glossary_page.Transvenose_description: (Of lamellae) provided with veins between the lamellae on the surface. (Vellinga, Fig. 30.1)
glossary_page.Trichoderm_description: A pileipellis made up of erect straight elements, septate, and/or not originating at the same level. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.3A)
glossary_page.Trichohymeniderm_description: A hymeniderm made up of elements with Q > 6. (Vellinga, Fig. 36.7)
glossary_page.Tricholomatoid_description: (Of habit) characterised by lamellae neither free, nor decurrent; stipe ± as long as pileus diameter; context fleshy; context of pileus continuous with stipe. (Vellinga, Figs. 28.9, 28.10, 28.11)
glossary_page.Truncate_description: Ending abruptly as if cut off. (Of spore apex: Vellinga, Fig. 33.29; of cystidia apex: Fig. 34.42)
glossary_page.Tubular_description: (Of hymenophore) consisting of tubes (as in boletes and polypores).
glossary_page.Type_specimen_description: A physical specimen (or illustration) that serves as the permanent reference point for the application of a species name. The physical anchor of a taxon name.
glossary_page.Umbilicate_description: (Of pileus) having a navel-like depression. (Vellinga, Fig. 29.27)
glossary_page.Umbonate_description: (Of pileus) with a broad rounded knob. (Vellinga, Fig. 29.33)
glossary_page.Undulate_description: (Of pileus margin) waved; minor undulation. (Vellinga, Fig. 29.43)
glossary_page.Utriform_description: (Of cystidia) characterised by neck broader than half the width of the cell body. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.28)
glossary_page.Vascular_hypha_description: Usually aseptate, often irregular, flexuose hyphal element with refractive contents; common in Russula and Lactarius.
glossary_page.Velutinous_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) = velvety; densely covered with fine, short, erect hairs.
glossary_page.Venose_description: (Of pileus surface) with vein-like wrinkles. See also reticulately venose.
glossary_page.Ventricose_description: (Of lamellae) with convex lamella edge; widest in the middle. (Vellinga, Figs. 30.11, 30.12, 30.13)
glossary_page.Verrucose_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces, spores) covered with wart-like elevations. (Vellinga, Fig. 34.46)
glossary_page.Villose_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) covered with fairly long, soft, ± straight, not interwoven hairs.
glossary_page.Virgate_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) streaked; with narrow lines of colour.
glossary_page.Viscid_description: (Of pileus and stipe surfaces) sticky, glutinous, or gelatinous to the touch when moist.
glossary_page.Volva_description: Remnant of the universal veil at the base of the stipe. Occurs in Amanita and some other genera. Types: saccate, circumsessile, marginately depressed, napiform, sheathing, friable-pulverulent, concentrically ringed, scaly.
glossary_page.Voucher_specimen_description: Any preserved specimen (herbarium, culture, DNA extract, photograph) that documents a biological observation and allows subsequent verification.
glossary_page.White_rot_description: Wood decay type in which both lignin and cellulose are degraded, leaving a white, fibrous residue. Many bioluminescent species (Mycena, Panellus, Omphalotus, Neonothopanus) are white-rot saprotrophs.
glossary_page.Width_spore_description: Largest distance between sides as seen in side view. (Vellinga, Fig. 32.1)
glossary_page.Wood_saprotroph_description: A saprotrophic fungus that decomposes dead wood (lignicolous). The most common primary lifestyle among basidiomycetes and the guild containing the majority of known bioluminescent species.
glossary_page.Zonate_description: (Of pileus surface) with concentric paler and darker alternating coloured zones or bands.

intro.text1: LUMM collects data on one of Earth's rarest biological phenomena: fungal bioluminescence. Every record in the system is a primary data point in an ongoing global census of ~132 known luminescent species. The quality and completeness of each record directly determines the scientific value of the database. This guide explains how to fill every field in the species record. It is intended for mycologists, naturalists, molecular biologists, and citizen scientists contributing observations. All contributions are reviewed by a curator before publication. Read this guide before your first submission.
intro.mandatory_note: Fields marked ★ are mandatory — a record without them cannot be published.

principles.title: General Principles
principles.item1: Use controlled vocabulary options first. Free-text notes fields are for data that genuinely cannot be captured by the available options — not a workaround to avoid choosing.
principles.item2: 'Unknown' is always preferable to a guess. A record with honest unknowns is scientifically useful; a record with plausible-sounding fabrications is dangerous.
principles.item3: Measure multiple specimens. For any range field, measure at least 5 mature basidiomes and report mean. A single measurement is not a range.

sec1.title: Section 1 – Observations
sec1.intro: Section 1 aggregates species occurrence data to visualize geographic distribution patterns through an interactive map interface. This feature integrates records from external biodiversity platforms, providing a consolidated view of field sightings.
sec1.text1: For the correct interpretation and use of this section, please consider the following points:
sec1.text2: Data Integration: The system consumes real-time data from iNaturalist, Mushroom Observer, and speciesLink platforms.
sec1.text3: Map Interactivity: Markers on the map represent individual sightings; clicking a marker displays an information pop-up containing the specimen photo, observation date, and identification status.
sec1.text4: Color Legend: The data source is visually identified by colors: Green markers represent iNaturalist, Orange represents Mushroom Observer, and Blue represents speciesLink.
sec1.text5: Access to Source: Each record includes a direct link (e.g., 'View on iNaturalist') that directs the user to the original platform, where detailed metadata and scientific community discussions can be reviewed.

sec2.title: Section 2 — Bioluminescent Anatomy
sec2.intro: Section 2 is LUMM's unique scientific contribution — no other database captures bioluminescence at this level of anatomical detail.
sec2.obs1: Allow your eyes to fully dark-adapt for at least 10–15 minutes in complete darkness before assessing luminescence.
sec2.obs2: Do not use a red torch — even dim red light resets adaptation.
sec2.obs3: Assess each anatomical structure independently (mycelium, pileus, lamellae, stipe) by covering the others.
sec2.obs4: Fresh, moist specimens luminesce most strongly.
sec2.obs5: Luminescence is suppressed by desiccation and cold (< 10 °C in many species).
sec2.photo1: Use a tripod and remote shutter.
sec2.photo2: Set ISO 3200–6400, aperture f/1.4–f/2.8 (widest available), and expose for 30–300 seconds depending on brightness.
sec2.photo3: Take one reference photograph in ambient light immediately beforehand to document colour and morphology.
sec2.photo4: Metadata to record: camera model, ISO, aperture, exposure duration, time after sunset, ambient temperature (°C), specimen age.
sec2.bio1: The gene accession fields (HispS, H3H, Luz, CPH, NpgA) require actual GenBank accession numbers from sequencing studies.
sec2.bio2: The biochemical pathway field 'Hispidin pathway — inferred from phylogeny' is appropriate for species closely related to characterised luminescent taxa without their own biochemical data.

sec3.title: Section 3 — Taxonomic Information
sec3.text1: Section 3 captures the species' full taxonomic identity.
sec3.text2: Always verify the accepted name against Mycobank and Index Fungorum before submission.
sec3.text3: MycoBank ID and Index Fungorum ID (these are different databases with same numbering system). This ID is mandatory for each species.

sec4.title: Section 4 — Ecological Attributes
sec4.intro: Section 4 links the species to its ecological role using FungalTraits nomenclature, enabling data exchange with GBIF, UNITE, and the global FungalTraits database.
sec4.hab_desc: Three separate fields capture habitat at different scales.
sec4.hab1: (1) Biome is the large climate zone — choose from the WWF/IUCN list.
sec4.hab2: (2) Vegetation/ecosystem type is the specific plant community within that biome.
sec4.hab3: (3) Microhabitat is the immediate physical situation of the fruiting body.
sec4.hab4: Substrate (what the fungus grows on) is a separate field in sub-section 3.2.
sec4.alt1: Record both the Altitude band (dropdown) and the exact Altitude in metres.
sec4.alt2: GPS elevation readings have error margins of ±10–30 m — note this in the habitat notes if relevant.
sec4.host1: Record the Latin name at the lowest taxonomic level you can confirm.
sec4.host2: 'Quercus' is better than 'oak'; 'Quercus robur' is best.

sec5.title: Section 5 — Conservation Status
sec5.intro: Section 5 presents the species' conservation status according to the IUCN Red List. This tool provides essential data on population, habitat, and threats to help guide necessary conservation decisions.
sec5.text1: The following fields are fundamental for correctly completing this section:
sec5.text2: Status: Indicates the species' risk category. The official abbreviations must be used: NE (Not Evaluated), DD (Data Deficient), LC (Least Concern), NT (Near Threatened), VU (Vulnerable), EN (Endangered), CR (Critically Endangered), EW (Extinct in the Wild), or EX (Extinct).
sec5.text3: Publication year: Refers to the year the latest official IUCN assessment was published and made available.
sec5.text4: IUCN assessment link: A direct URL to the species' page on the IUCN Red List portal, allowing for detailed verification of field data and category justifications.

sec6.title: Section 6 — Molecular Data
sec6.text2: The NCBI Taxonomy ID is the anchor — entering it allows LUMM to auto-generate links to every Entrez database.
sec6.text3: For bioluminescent species, the gene cluster fields (HispS, H3H, Luz, CPH, NpgA) are especially important: enter GenBank accession numbers only when the sequence was obtained from THIS species, not from a related organism used as a reference.

sec7.title: Section 7 — References & Media
sec7.text2: For bibliography, include all papers that describe this species' bioluminescence — even early or preliminary reports.
sec7.text3: For media, always upload both a standard ambient-light photograph and a long-exposure bioluminescence photograph when possible — the contrast between the two is scientifically and visually informative.

photo_guide.title: Bioluminescence Photography Guide
photo_guide.cam1: ISO: 3200–12800 (higher for faint bioluminescence; balance against noise)
photo_guide.cam3: Exposure duration: 30 s (bright specimens) to 5 min (faint specimens)
photo_guide.cam4: Tripod required. Use a remote shutter or self-timer to avoid camera shake.
photo_guide.cam5: Mirror lock-up (DSLR) or electronic front-curtain shutter (mirrorless) recommended for long exposures.
photo_guide.prot1: Take a reference photograph in ambient or artificial light first, documenting colour and morphology.
photo_guide.prot2: Allow eyes to dark-adapt for 10–15 minutes before visual assessment. Do not use any light source during adaptation.
photo_guide.prot3: Use ISO preview to check luminescence distribution before committing to a full exposure.
photo_guide.prot4: For multiple specimens: photograph all under identical conditions for intensity comparison.
photo_guide.meta_title: Required metadata for every bioluminescence image
photo_guide.meta1: Camera make & model / Lens focal length / ISO / Aperture (f-stop) / Exposure duration (seconds)
photo_guide.meta2: Date and time (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM local time, noting time zone)
photo_guide.meta3: Minutes after sunset or civil twilight
photo_guide.meta5: Life stage of specimen (young / mature / old)
photo_guide.meta6: GPS coordinates of collection site (decimal degrees, WGS84)
photo_guide.ethics1: Collect only the minimum number of specimens needed. Prioritise photography in situ without collecting when possible.
photo_guide.ethics2: Follow all applicable national and regional collection regulations.
photo_guide.ethics3: Do not use flash or any light source on adjacent specimens during long-exposure photography — this can disturb the documentation of other luminescent structures.
